lessons.title: Exact output with print
lessons.body: print converts each argument, inserts sep between arguments, and writes end afterward. Online judges compare stdout exactly, so an extra space or missing newline is observable behavior.
lessons.example:
name = "Mina"
score = 9
print(name, score, sep="=", end="!\n")
lessons.starter:
import sys

name, score = sys.stdin.read().split()
# TODO: choose print separators and a line ending for the required record
print(name, score)

lessons.title: Read stdin once
lessons.body: sys.stdin.read returns the complete input as text. Splitting without an explicit separator accepts spaces, newlines, and repeated whitespace; an empty stream produces no tokens and therefore a zero sum.
lessons.example:
raw = "10\n4"
values = [int(token) for token in raw.split()]
print(sum(values))
lessons.starter:
import sys

raw = sys.stdin.read()
# TODO: convert every whitespace-delimited token instead of only the first
values = [int(token) for token in raw.split()[:1]]
print(sum(values))

lessons.title: Names and rebinding
lessons.body: Assignment binds a name to an object; augmented assignment then rebinds the accumulator to the computed integer. The original integer is unchanged because integers are immutable, while closures may later read the current value stored in an enclosing cell.
lessons.example:
total = 10
total += 5
print(total)
lessons.starter:
import sys

left, right = map(int, sys.stdin.read().split())
total = left
# TODO: rebind the accumulator using the second parsed value
print(total)

lessons.title: Floor quotient and remainder
lessons.body: For a nonzero divisor, integer // rounds the quotient toward negative infinity and % supplies the paired remainder. That differs from truncating division: negative operands can make the quotient one smaller, and the remainder follows the divisor's sign.
lessons.example:
dividend = -11
divisor = 4
print(f"{dividend // divisor}:{dividend % divisor}")
lessons.starter:
import sys

dividend, divisor = map(int, sys.stdin.read().split())
# TODO: compute the integer quotient with Python's floor rule
print(f"{dividend / divisor}:{dividend % divisor}")

lessons.title: Ordered pairs and unique members
lessons.body: A tuple preserves the two input positions, while set(pair) retains one instance of each hashable value. Empty braces create a dict rather than a set, so an empty set must be written as set().
lessons.example:
pair = ("r", "s", "r")
unique = set(pair)
print("".join(pair), len(unique))
lessons.starter:
import sys

pair = tuple(sys.stdin.read().split())
# TODO: derive the unique members from the ordered pair
unique = set()
print("".join(pair), len(unique))

lessons.title: Unicode-aware string slicing
lessons.body: Python str values are immutable sequences of Unicode code points. A half-open slice can remove the first and last code point without altering the source, including when those boundary values are emoji.
lessons.example:
wrapped = "★서울★"
print(wrapped[1:-1])
lessons.starter:
import sys

text = sys.stdin.read().rstrip("\n")
# TODO: slice away both boundary code points
print(text[1:])

lessons.title: Path components with pathlib
lessons.body: PurePosixPath parses POSIX path structure without touching the filesystem. stem removes only the final suffix, suffix reports that final extension, and a leading-dot filename such as .env has no suffix.
lessons.example:
from pathlib import PurePosixPath

path = PurePosixPath("/srv/report.csv")
print(f"{path.stem}:{path.suffix}")
lessons.starter:
import sys
from pathlib import PurePosixPath

path = PurePosixPath(sys.stdin.read().strip())
# TODO: report the final stem and final suffix as separate properties
print(path.name)

lessons.title: Branches, loops, and range bounds
lessons.body: Indentation determines which statements belong to a loop or branch. range excludes its stop, so including n requires n + 1; the condition then selects only odd values before they reach the accumulator.
lessons.starter:
import sys

limit = int(sys.stdin.read().strip())
total = 0
for value in range(1, limit + 1):
    # TODO: select the parity requested by the exercise
    if value % 2 == 0:
        total += value
print(total)

lessons.title: Checkpoint: return a computed area
lessons.body: A function boundary separates parsing, calculation, and output. Parameters receive the two dimensions, return hands the product back to the caller, and only the outer print writes judge-visible text.
lessons.starter:
import sys

def area(width, height):
    # TODO: return the rectangle calculation to the caller
    return width + height

width, height = map(int, sys.stdin.read().split())
print(area(width, height))

lessons.title: Visible dependencies through modules
lessons.body: import math binds the module namespace, making math.ceil's origin explicit at the call site. Star imports hide where names came from and make collisions harder to review.
lessons.example:
import math

value = -3.8
print(math.ceil(value))
lessons.starter:
import math
import sys

value = float(sys.stdin.read().strip())
# TODO: call the upward-rounding operation through the module namespace
print(math.floor(value))

lessons.title: Closures capture cells, not snapshots
lessons.body: A nested function can read a binding from an enclosing scope after that outer call returns. The captured cell is resolved when the closure runs, which is why lambdas created by one loop commonly all observe the loop's final value.
lessons.example:
readers = [lambda: index for index in range(3)]
print([reader() for reader in readers])
lessons.starter:
import sys

def make_adder(delta):
    # TODO: return a closure that uses the enclosing binding
    return lambda value: value

delta, value = map(int, sys.stdin.read().split())
print(make_adder(delta)(value))

lessons.title: Definition-time decorators
lessons.body: Decorator expressions run when the def statement executes, while the returned wrapper runs on each later call. functools.wraps copies identifying metadata so tooling still sees the wrapped function's name and documentation.

lessons.title: Drive real coroutines with asyncio
lessons.body: Calling an async function creates a coroutine object but does not schedule it. asyncio.run drives the top-level coroutine, gather schedules the child awaitables, and sleep(0) provides an actual suspension point while preserving result order.
lessons.example:
import asyncio

async def upper(word):
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    return word.upper()

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(upper("go"), upper("now"))
    print(*results)

asyncio.run(main())
lessons.starter:
import asyncio
import sys

async def double(value):
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    return value * 2

async def main():
    values = [int(token) for token in sys.stdin.read().split()]
    # TODO: gather and await one double coroutine for each parsed value
    results = values
    print(*results)

asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: Group list values by dictionary key
lessons.body: Lists retain every score in insertion order, while a dict maps each name to its own list. setdefault can create a fresh list per missing key, and get handles a query that never appeared without raising KeyError.
lessons.starter:
import sys

tokens = sys.stdin.read().split()
query = tokens[0]
scores = {}
# TODO: group every following name-score pair, not only the first one
name, score = tokens[1], int(tokens[2])
scores.setdefault(name, []).append(score)
print(sum(scores.get(query, [])))

lessons.title: Multi-field sorting keys
lessons.body: A key tuple can encode descending score as a negated number and ascending name as the second field. Python's sort is stable, but stability alone preserves input order on ties rather than inventing the required alphabetical rule.
lessons.example:
users = [("Mina", 7), ("Ana", 7), ("Bo", 9)]
best = sorted(users, key=lambda item: (-item[1], item[0]))[0]
print(f"{best[0]}:{best[1]}")
lessons.starter:
import sys

users = []
for token in sys.stdin.read().split():
    name, score = token.rsplit(":", 1)
    users.append((name, int(score)))
# TODO: rank by the numeric result before applying the tie-break
best = sorted(users, key=lambda item: item[0])[0]
print(f"{best[0]}:{best[1]}")

lessons.title: Count and group with collections
lessons.body: Counter records frequencies of hashable values, and defaultdict(list) supplies a fresh bucket while grouping. Indexing a missing defaultdict key creates that entry, whereas get can inspect an absent group without mutating the mapping.
lessons.example:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

query = "owl"
words = ["oak", "owl", "ox", "owl", "otter"]
counts = Counter(words)
groups = defaultdict(list)
for word in words:
    groups[word[0]].append(word)
print(counts[query], len(groups.get(query[0], [])))
lessons.starter:
import sys
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

query, *words = sys.stdin.read().split()
# TODO: count and group every word instead of only the first one
counts = Counter(words[:1])
groups = defaultdict(list)
if words:
    groups[words[0][0]].append(words[0])
print(counts[query], len(groups.get(query[0], [])))

lessons.title: Constant-time operations at both ends
lessons.body: collections.deque supports append, appendleft, pop, and popleft at opposite ends. A list can pop efficiently from its right, but removing index zero shifts the remaining elements and is not the equivalent queue operation.
lessons.example:
from collections import deque

queue = deque(["center"])
queue.appendleft("first")
queue.append("last")
print(queue.popleft(), queue.pop())
lessons.starter:
import sys
from collections import deque

middle, left, right = sys.stdin.read().split()
queue = deque([middle])
# TODO: place the second token on the opposite end from the third
queue.append(left)
queue.append(right)
print(queue.popleft(), queue.pop())

lessons.title: Checkpoint: filter, transform, then total
lessons.body: A list comprehension reads from left to right as an output expression, an iteration clause, and an optional filter. It eagerly builds the selected squares; a generator expression would instead feed a lazy, single-pass pipeline.
lessons.example:
numbers = [5, 6, 7, 8]
even_squares = [number * number for number in numbers if number % 2 == 0]
print(sum(even_squares))
lessons.starter:
import sys

numbers = [int(token) for token in sys.stdin.read().split()]
# TODO: keep the requested parity while building squared values
squares = [number * number for number in numbers if number % 2 != 0]
print(sum(squares))

lessons.title: Lazy countdown generators
lessons.body: Calling a generator function returns a generator object before its body executes. Each advance runs until yield, saves the suspended state, and resumes later; exhausting the iterator produces no more values.
lessons.starter:
import sys

def countdown(number):
    # TODO: continue yielding while decreasing toward the stopping boundary
    if number > 0:
        yield number

number = int(sys.stdin.read().strip())
print(*countdown(number))

lessons.title: Flatten iterables lazily
lessons.body: itertools.chain.from_iterable visits each inner iterable in sequence without first copying every element into a new list. The resulting iterator is single-pass, so a second consumer sees only whatever remains.
lessons.example:
from itertools import chain

groups = ("py", "thon")
print("".join(chain.from_iterable(groups)))
lessons.starter:
import sys
from itertools import chain

groups = sys.stdin.read().split()
# TODO: let the chain consume every inner character group
characters = chain.from_iterable(groups[:1])
print("".join(characters))

lessons.title: Recover narrowly from ValueError
lessons.body: Only conversion of the first token belongs inside the try block. Catching ValueError selects the supplied fallback for malformed numeric text without swallowing unrelated programming errors.
lessons.example:
candidate = "oops"
fallback = "-8"
try:
    value = int(candidate)
except ValueError:
    value = int(fallback)
print(value)
lessons.starter:
import sys

candidate, fallback = sys.stdin.read().split()
try:
    value = int(candidate)
except ValueError:
    # TODO: recover from conversion using the supplied token
    value = 0
print(value)

lessons.title: Guaranteed context-manager exit
lessons.body: The with statement calls __enter__, runs the managed block, and invokes __exit__ on normal or exceptional departure. StringIO supplies file-like behavior in memory, allowing the exercise to observe resource reading without filesystem state.
lessons.example:
from io import StringIO

with StringIO("  Seoul  ") as handle:
    text = handle.read().strip()
print(text.upper() or "EMPTY")
lessons.starter:
import sys
from io import StringIO

with StringIO(sys.stdin.read()) as handle:
    text = handle.read()
    # TODO: strip surrounding whitespace before normalizing the managed text
print(text.upper() or "EMPTY")

lessons.title: Classes and generated data-model methods
lessons.body: A class defines behavior shared by instances, and @dataclass generates field-based construction, representation, and equality. Generated methods do not freeze mutable fields or enforce annotations at runtime; explicit methods still carry domain behavior.
lessons.starter:
import sys
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int

    def total(self):
        # TODO: combine both instance fields in this behavior
        return self.x

x, y = map(int, sys.stdin.read().split())
point = Point(x, y)
print(f"{point.total()}:{point == Point(x, y)}")

lessons.title: Inspect annotations without runtime enforcement
lessons.body: Annotations describe a callable contract for readers and static tools, but Python does not automatically reject mismatched argument values. get_type_hints resolves the recorded annotations; the function body must still perform the promised calculation.
lessons.starter:
import sys
from typing import Iterable, get_type_hints

def total(values: Iterable[int]) -> int:
    # TODO: total the complete iterable instead of returning only its first item
    return next(iter(values), 0)

values = [int(token) for token in sys.stdin.read().split()]
hints = get_type_hints(total)
label = "typed" if hints.get("return") is int and "values" in hints else "untyped"
print(f"{label}:{total(values)}")

lessons.title: Capstone: deterministic word ranking
lessons.body: The capstone combines typed functions, Counter, and an explicit sort key to select the highest frequency with an alphabetical tie-break. Internal assert statements document development invariants, but Python removes assert bytecode under -O, so assertions must never protect input, security, or data integrity.
lessons.example:
from collections import Counter
from typing import Iterable

def most_common(words: Iterable[str]) -> tuple[str, int]:
    materialized = list(words)
    counts = Counter(materialized)
    assert sum(counts.values()) == len(materialized)
    if not counts:
        return "empty", 0
    return sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda item: (-item[1], item[0]))[0]

word, count = most_common("pear plum pear apple plum".split())
print(f"{word}:{count}")
lessons.starter:
import sys
from collections import Counter
from typing import Iterable

def most_common(words: Iterable[str]) -> tuple[str, int]:
    materialized = list(words)
    counts = Counter(materialized)
    assert sum(counts.values()) == len(materialized)
    if not counts:
        return "empty", 0
    # TODO: make equal frequencies deterministic with the required ranking rule
    return counts.most_common(1)[0]

word, count = most_common(sys.stdin.read().split())
print(f"{word}:{count}")
